lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: I'm sorry to bother you, but could you spare me a little dung?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: If I go near that camel, it'll probably bite my hand off.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Mmm... looks like that camel would make a nice kebab.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: I wonder if that camel has fleas...

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: I'm sorry to bother you, but could you spare me a little dung?

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: Are you serious?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Oh yes. If you'd be so kind...

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: I'm sorry, but I can't really help you right now. Perhaps you should ask me again later, or try a different camel.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Fair enough.

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: Well, just you close your eyes first. I'm not doing it while you're watching me!

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: I hope that's what you wanted!

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Ohhh yes. Lovely.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: If I go near that camel, it'll probably bite my hand off.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Mmm... looks like that camel would make a nice kebab.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: I wonder if that camel has fleas...

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: Sorry, were you saying something to me?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: No, er, nothing important.

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: Never mind, it is unimportant when I have such important matters weighing on my soul.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: How important can a camel's problems be?

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: Well, you see, there is a camel called Elly. A beautiful, wondrous camel, with hide like spun gold and teeth that shine like an oasis.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: ...I see.

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: I've written many poems describing her beauty. Would you like to hear one?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: It's all right, I'm...

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: Shall I compare thee to a desert's day? Thou art drier and more rough-skinned.

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: Rough sandstorms shake the cactuses away And summer's heat defers to autumn wind.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Look, I don't really have the time to...

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: Sometimes too hot the eye of heaven shines, With Guthix' gold complexion often dimmed;

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: And every fair from fair sometime declines, By chance or desert's changing course untrimmed...

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Please, stop.

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: But I've only got six lines left!

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: That's six too many. If I really want to hear your poetry, I'll tell you, okay?

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: Very well. Come back and talk to me if you want to hear more.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: If I go near that camel, it'll probably bite my hand off.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Mmm... looks like that camel would make a nice kebab.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: I wonder if that camel has fleas...

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: Oh, it's you again. Have you come back to listen to my poems?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: I'd love to!

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: That's so kind of you. Which one would you like to hear?

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: 'Shall I compare thee to a desert's day' is my finest yet, but I've also composed others.

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: That's my favourite poem. Ahem...

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: Shall I compare thee to a desert's day? Thou art drier and more rough-skinned.

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: Rough sandstorms shake the cactuses away And summer's heat defers to autumn wind.

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: Sometimes too hot the eye of heaven shines, With Guthix' gold complexion often dimmed;

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: And every fair from fair sometime declines, By chance or desert's changing course untrimmed...

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: But thine eternal desert shall not fade Nor lose possession of that sand thou owest;

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: Nor Zamorak brag thou art in his shades, When in eternal lines to sand thou growest,

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: So long as camels breathe or eyes can see, So long lives this, and this gives life to thee.

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: Ah, Elly, how beautiful you are.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: What does Elly think of your poems?

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: She's never heard them.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Why not?

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: I suspect she loves another - Ollie, another camel who roams with her to the north.

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: So I shall stay here and compose poems for her.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Why not tell her how you feel?

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: She seems happy enough as she is, and I have my poems to comfort me.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Well, best of luck with that.

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: If you want to hear my poems once more, please come back again.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Well, best of luck with that.

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: If you want to hear my poems once more, please come back again.

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: I wrote this poem when I went to the oasis to nibble at a tree, then discovered I'd left nothing for Elly to nibble. I was distraught.

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: This Is Just To Say

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: I have nibbled the cacti that were by the oasis,

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: and which you were probably saving for lunch.

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: Forgive me, they were delicious, so crunchy and so cold.

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: I wonder if she's forgiven me for eating her snack.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: What does Elly think of your poems?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: No, thank you.

lines.speaker: Al the Camel
lines.text: Ah, well. I shall return to writing poems to Elly's beauty.
